title: Quantization
lesson:
Quantization means storing a model's numbers with less precision so the whole model takes up far less space and runs faster. That is the entire idea. Everything else is detail. A model is just a giant pile of numbers called parameters (the learned weights that turn your input into output). Normally each number is stored in high precision, using lots of bits. Quantization rounds them off and stores them with fewer bits — trading a little accuracy for a huge saving in memory and speed.

The two key words: bits and precision

A bit is a single 0 or 1. The more bits you spend per number, the finer the detail you can record. Models are usually trained in 16-bit precision (often called FP16 or BF16, where FP means "floating point" — a number with a decimal point). Quantization shrinks each number down to 8 bits, 4 bits, or even fewer.

The format you store numbers in is called the data type, and the number of bits is the bit-width. Why does this matter so much? Because model size is roughly bytes per number times number of parameters:

  • A 7-billion-parameter model in 16-bit needs ~14 GB just to sit in memory.
  • Drop it to 4-bit and it needs ~3.5 GB — small enough for a laptop or a cheap GPU.

This is the difference between "needs a data-centre" and "runs on the machine in front of you," which is why quantization underpins Local Inference and tools like Ollama and llama.cpp.

Analogy one: rounding a shopping bill

Imagine a long shopping list with exact prices: 3.4271, 1.9982, 5.0413. To add them quickly in your head, you round to whole pounds: 3, 2, 5. You lose a few pennies of accuracy, but the answer is almost right and you got it ten times faster. Quantization rounds the model's numbers the same way — most of the time the rounded answer is good enough, and occasionally the rounding error shows up as a slightly worse response.

Analogy two: image file sizes

A RAW photo from a camera is huge and stores every subtle shade. A JPEG of the same photo is a fraction of the size and looks nearly identical — until you zoom right in and see fuzzy edges. A 16-bit model is the RAW file; a 4-bit model is the JPEG. For most everyday use you cannot tell the difference, but push it hard and the compression artefacts (the small errors from squeezing it down) start to show.

How the rounding actually works

You cannot just chop bits off blindly, because the numbers in a model span a wide range. Instead, quantization finds the smallest and largest value in a group, then maps that range onto the few values the new format can hold. The conversion factor it uses is called the scale.

Smart methods keep one scale per small block of numbers rather than one for a whole layer, so a single huge value can't ruin the rounding for everyone else. Methods you will see named — GPTQ, AWQ, and the GGUF formats (see GGUF Models) — are all clever recipes for choosing those scales so accuracy barely drops.

Two places it happens

  • Post-training quantization (PTQ): train normally in full precision, then squash the finished model. This is by far the most common, and what you do when you download a "4-bit" model.
  • Quantization-aware training (QAT): the model practises with the rounding switched on during training, so it learns to cope. More accuracy, more effort.

A close cousin is QLoRA, which keeps a frozen 4-bit base model and trains a small high-precision LoRA add-on on top — cheap fine-tuning without paying full memory.

What it looks like in code

With Hugging Face you can load a model in 4-bit in a few lines:

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig

quant = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,            # store weights in 4 bits
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",   # a 4-bit type tuned for model weights
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype="bfloat16",  # do the math in 16-bit
)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B", quantization_config=quant
)

Notice the math (compute_dtype) still happens in 16-bit even though the weights are stored in 4-bit. The weights are unpacked to higher precision just before each calculation. This split — store small, compute bigger — is the trick that keeps quality high while saving memory. The big saving is in VRAM (the memory on your graphics card), which is usually the thing that runs out first.

16-bit (full)4-bit (quantized)
~14 GB for a 7B model~3.5 GB for a 7B model
Best possible qualitySlightly lower, usually unnoticeable
Needs a big GPURuns on a laptop or cheap GPU
Slower to load and move aroundFaster to load, often faster to run

💡 For everyday local use, 4-bit is the sweet spot. 8-bit is nearly lossless if you can spare the memory; go below 4-bit only if you must.
mentalModel: Quantization is saving a model as a JPEG instead of a RAW photo: you round every number to fewer bits, so the file shrinks dramatically and loads fast, while looking almost identical until you push it really hard.
takeaways: Quantization stores weights in fewer bits — smaller, faster, slightly less accurate.
4-bit roughly quarters a model's size; the sweet spot for local use.
Weights stored small, but math still computed in 16-bit precision.
mistakes: Assuming all 4-bit models are equal. A naive round-everything-the-same 4-bit model can be noticeably worse than one made with a smart method like GPTQ, AWQ, or a good GGUF type. The recipe matters as much as the bit-width.

Going too low and blaming the model. 2-bit and 3-bit models often degrade sharply — garbled output, broken reasoning. If quality tanks, suspect over-aggressive quantization before you blame the base model.
Confusing storage precision with compute precision. Weights stored in 4-bit are usually unpacked to 16-bit for the actual math. "4-bit" describes how it is stored, not how every calculation runs.

Expecting huge speedups automatically. Quantization mainly saves memory. You get speed too, but only if the software has fast code (kernels) for that exact format on your hardware — otherwise unpacking can even slow things down.
Quantizing a model you still need to train fully. If you plan heavy fine-tuning, train in higher precision (or use QLoRA). Squashing first then training the whole thing in 4-bit usually goes badly.

Ignoring the KV cache. The model's running memory during generation (the KV Cache) also takes space and can be quantized separately. People shrink the weights, then run out of memory anyway on long contexts.
exercise:
Pick a small open model (for example an 8B Llama or Qwen) and load it twice on the same machine: once in full 16-bit and once in 4-bit using BitsAndBytesConfig(load_in_4bit=True). After each load, print the GPU memory used (in PyTorch: torch.cuda.memory_allocated() / 1e9 for gigabytes). Write down both numbers and the rough ratio between them.

Stretch goal: Give both versions the same 5 prompts (a maths question, a summary, a coding task, a factual question, and a creative one) and compare the answers side by side. Note which prompt types, if any, the 4-bit version handles visibly worse.
solution: Hint: You should see the 4-bit model use roughly a quarter of the memory of the 16-bit one — for an 8B model, very roughly 5 GB versus around 16 GB (numbers vary with overhead and the KV cache). On the prompts, the 4-bit answers will usually look nearly identical for chat, summaries, and factual recall. Differences are most likely to surface on tight reasoning or maths, where small rounding errors compound. If 4-bit looks badly broken across the board, check that bnb_4bit_compute_dtype is set to bfloat16 (not left at default) and that you are using the nf4 quant type, which is tuned for weights.
summary: Quantization stores a model's numbers with fewer bits (16-bit down to 8, 4, or fewer), shrinking memory and speeding things up while losing a little accuracy. 4-bit roughly quarters the size of a model and is the sweet spot for running on everyday hardware. Smart methods (GPTQ, AWQ, GGUF) keep quality high, weights are stored small but computed in higher precision, and going below 4-bit is where quality usually breaks.

keyTerms.def: Storing a model's numbers with fewer bits to save memory and gain speed, at the cost of a small amount of accuracy.
A single 0 or 1. More bits per number means finer detail; quantization spends fewer bits per number.
How many bits are used to store each number. 16-bit is high precision; 4-bit is low precision (more compressed).
The format a number is stored in, such as FP16, BF16, INT8, or NF4. It sets the bit-width and the range of values allowed.
The conversion factor that maps a model's wide range of real values onto the few values a low-bit format can hold.
Quantizing a model after it is fully trained. The common case: this is what a downloaded '4-bit model' usually is.
Training the model with the rounding switched on so it learns to tolerate low precision, keeping more accuracy for more effort.
Popular quantization recipes and file formats that choose scales cleverly so accuracy barely drops at low bit-widths.
The precision used for the actual math, often 16-bit, even when the weights are stored in 4-bit and unpacked just before each calculation.

diagram.caption: 16-bit weights are rounded and scaled down to 4-bit (about a quarter of the size), then briefly unpacked back to 16-bit for the actual math.

quiz.q: What does quantization mainly change about a model?
options: How many parameters the model has
How many bits are used to store each number
Which dataset the model was trained on
The number of layers in the network
quiz.explain: Quantization keeps the same parameters but stores each one in fewer bits (e.g. 16-bit down to 4-bit), shrinking memory at a small cost to accuracy.

quiz.q: Roughly how much memory does a 7B model need in 4-bit versus 16-bit?
options: About the same, ~14 GB either way
quiz.explain: Going from 16 bits to 4 bits is a 4x reduction in bytes per number, so the ~14 GB model drops to roughly 3.5 GB — small enough for a laptop or cheap GPU.

quiz.q: A friend says a '4-bit' model means every calculation is done in 4-bit math. Why is that wrong?
options: Quantization only affects training, not inference
4-bit describes storage; weights are unpacked to ~16-bit for the actual math
4-bit models cannot run on a GPU at all
The compute always happens in 2-bit to save even more
quiz.explain: "4-bit" is how weights are stored. They are unpacked to a higher precision (the compute dtype, often bfloat16) just before each calculation — store small, compute bigger.

quiz.q: Your 4-bit model gives garbled, broken answers across the board. What is the most likely cause?
options: The base model is simply bad and should be replaced
Over-aggressive quantization, or a misconfigured quant type / compute dtype
The GPU has too much VRAM available
The model has too few parameters to quantize
quiz.explain: Widespread breakage usually means quantization went too far (e.g. 2–3 bit) or settings like the quant type and compute dtype are wrong — suspect the recipe before blaming the base model.
